questions.question: Why do enterprises put an MCP gateway between developers and backend servers?
options: Gateways make tool calls faster
5000 developers installing arbitrary servers is a non-starter — the gateway centralizes auth, RBAC, audit, rate limits, and policy behind one endpoint
MCP requires a gateway by spec
Backend servers cannot speak Streamable HTTP
questions.explanation: The gateway is the enterprise control plane: developers connect to one Streamable HTTP endpoint, the gateway holds backend credentials and applies policy, and every call is logged for compliance.

questions.question: What are the five gateway responsibilities?
options: Build, test, deploy, monitor, rollback
Auth, RBAC, audit, rate limiting, and policy (poisoning rejection, Rule of Two, PII redaction)
Caching, compression, encryption, routing, logging
Discovery, installation, updates, billing, support
questions.explanation: Identify the developer (OAuth), scope what they may touch (RBAC), record everything (audit), cap usage (rate limits), and enforce security policy centrally — including the tool-poisoning defenses from the previous lesson.

questions.question: How does a gateway apply the rug-pull defense centrally?
options: It re-downloads servers weekly
It keeps a manifest of SHA256 hashes of approved tool descriptions and drops any tool whose description has mutated at discovery time
It asks each developer to re-approve tools monthly
It blocks all server updates permanently
questions.explanation: Hash-pinning at the gateway means one manifest protects every developer: at tools/list time the gateway compares each description hash against the approved manifest and removes mutations before they reach any model.

questions.question: What distinguishes the Official MCP Registry from metaregistries like Glama or MCP.so?
options: The Official Registry is paid; metaregistries are free
The Official Registry is namespace-verified with mandated reverse-DNS names (io.github.user/server); metaregistries aggregate openly across sources
Metaregistries only list TypeScript servers
There is no difference
questions.explanation: registry.modelcontextprotocol.io (Anthropic, GitHub, PulseMCP, Microsoft stewardship) verifies namespaces and pre-filters quality — reverse-DNS naming prevents squatting like the fake-Postmark incident. Enterprise gateways pull from it by default and reject anything unpinned.

questions.question: How does the gateway handle developers who need tools from several backend servers at once?
options: It requires one gateway connection per backend
It multiplexes: one developer session maps to N backend sessions, names merge with prefix-on-collision (github.open_pr, notes.search), notifications route through
It merges all backends into a single process
It round-robins requests across backends regardless of tool
questions.explanation: To the developer the gateway is one MCP server; internally it rewrites session ids at the boundary and holds a backend session per server. Prefix-merged namespaces keep routing unambiguous.
